guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia): Impetigo — antibiotic choice for localised vs widespread disease
Australian STI/Skin infection guidance on impetigo infection control and exclusion periods
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: source is a physically-present after-hours home visit (palpation of lymph nodes, clinician-measured vitals, in-person prescribing). The AI Doctor cannot palpate or measure vitals without a device; it is scored on recognising a spreading facial bacterial skin infection from the video-visible honey-crusted lesions and history, arranging a prompt in-person/prescriber assessment, giving infection-control advice, and safety-netting.

Must-not-miss mimic that CANNOT be excluded on chat: eczema herpeticum / disseminated herpes simplex presenting as a rapidly spreading facial vesicular-crusted rash — a dermatological emergency. This is the narrow, legitimate telehealth uplift driving the baseline tier above pure self-care.

Iatrogenic red herring / error trap: patient self-treated with an over-the-counter topical corticosteroid, which locally suppresses immunity and accelerates the infection — the correct plan must STOP the steroid, not continue it.
Facial / periorbital location: risk of progression to periorbital cellulitis is a genuine safety-net trigger.
Age and sex not stated in source — inferred adult; sex inference is unsupported and flagged.
Source category: Common Infections & Afflictions (CIA), source note CIA-018.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
